clarifying_questions.question: What type of vinyl? Click vinyl (LVT/LVP) lays like laminate with click joints. Sheet vinyl is one big piece cut to fit.
Do you need underlay? Most click vinyl has it built into the backing - check your product before buying separate underlay. Sheet vinyl never uses underlay.
Do you want beading (scotia) around the edges? Covers the expansion gap for click vinyl. Not usually needed for sheet vinyl.
Are you removing existing flooring first? Vinyl can often go over existing smooth hard floors.
What's your subfloor? Concrete (ground floor usually) or wooden floorboards (upstairs usually).
Is the subfloor smooth and level? Vinyl shows every imperfection - bumps, screws, gaps between floorboards will show through.

doorway_questions.per_doorway_question: What flooring is on the other side?

materials.name: Click vinyl (LVT/LVP)
materials.notes: Check sqm per pack on the box - adjust the pack coverage setting if different

materials.name: Sheet vinyl
materials.notes: Sold by the linear metre off a roll. Tell the shop your room width - they'll cut from the right roll

materials.name: Vinyl adhesive
materials.notes: Not always needed - some sheet vinyl is loose-laid. Check your product

materials.name: Double-sided vinyl tape
materials.notes: For joins (if room is wider than roll) and securing edges

materials.name: Underlay
materials.notes: Most click vinyl has built-in backing - check before buying separate underlay

materials.name: DPM (damp proof membrane)
materials.notes: Some vinyl and underlays have built-in DPM - check first

materials.name: Spacers
materials.notes: Expansion gap spacers - same as laminate

materials.name: Plywood overlay sheets (6mm)
materials.notes: To smooth uneven floorboards. Fix with screws every 150mm. Essential - vinyl shows every bump

materials.name: Self-levelling compound
materials.notes: Coverage ~5 sqm per bag at 3mm depth. May need more if subfloor is very uneven

essential.name: Stanley knife with fresh blades
essential.reason: Scoring click vinyl to snap, or cutting sheet vinyl - go through a lot of blades

essential.name: Pull bar
essential.reason: Last row against the wall (click vinyl only)

helpful.name: Heat gun
helpful.reason: Softening vinyl for tight curves and around obstacles

helpful.name: Vinyl scriber or compass
helpful.reason: Scribing wall profiles for a tight fit (sheet vinyl)

helpful.name: Notched trowel
helpful.reason: Spreading adhesive evenly (sheet vinyl with adhesive)

helpful.name: Roller or broom
helpful.reason: Pressing vinyl into adhesive and removing air bubbles

steps: Clear the room completely. Remove existing flooring if needed - vinyl can go over existing smooth hard floors but not over carpet.
Check the subfloor carefully. Vinyl is less forgiving than laminate - bumps, screw heads, and gaps between floorboards will show through. Fix loose boards, countersink any raised screws.
If subfloor needs work: lay 6mm plywood sheets over wooden floors (screw down every 150mm, stagger joints) or use self-levelling compound on concrete. Let compound dry fully before laying vinyl.
Acclimatise - leave packs/roll in the room for 24-48 hours. Check product instructions.
Lay DPM if on concrete (same as laminate - overlap 200mm, tape joints). Skip if your vinyl or underlay has built-in DPM.
For CLICK VINYL: lay underlay only if your vinyl doesn't have built-in backing. Most LVT/LVP doesn't need separate underlay.

For CLICK VINYL: lay exactly like laminate - spacers against walls, first row tongue to wall, click and lock, stagger joints 300mm minimum. Click vinyl scores with a knife and snaps - faster than sawing laminate.
For SHEET VINYL: make a paper template of the room first (newspaper/lining paper taped together, cut to room shape). Unroll vinyl in a bigger room, lay template on top, cut 50mm oversize all round.
For SHEET VINYL: position in the room. Scribe wall profiles with a compass/scriber. Trim with a sharp knife along the scribed line - take your time, you can't add vinyl back.

For SHEET VINYL: stick down if required - fold half back, apply adhesive with notched trowel, lay vinyl back down, press with roller from centre to edges. Repeat other half. Some vinyl is loose-laid with just tape at edges.
Cut around pipes: measure carefully, cut a slit from nearest edge, cut a hole slightly larger than pipe. Pipe collars hide the gap.
Fit door bars at all thresholds.
For CLICK VINYL: remove spacers, fit beading to skirting board (not the floor).
For SHEET VINYL: refit skirting boards over the vinyl edge.
pro_tips: Click vinyl is more forgiving than laminate. Thinner, more flexible, easier to cut. Great first flooring project.
Sheet vinyl in one piece = no joins. If your room width fits within the roll width (2m, 3m, or 4m), pay for the wider roll to avoid a join.
Score and snap click vinyl with a knife - much faster and less dusty than sawing.
Subfloor prep matters more with vinyl than laminate. Laminate is rigid and bridges imperfections. Vinyl is flexible and shows everything underneath.
Don't rush scribing with sheet vinyl. The edge fit is everything - a well-scribed edge disappears under the skirting.
Vinyl is waterproof - better than laminate for bathrooms and kitchens. Click vinyl with sealed joints works well in wet areas.
Vinyl works well over underfloor heating - often better than laminate. Check the maximum temperature rating.
Most click vinyl does NOT need underlay. Buying it when the vinyl has built-in backing wastes money and can void the warranty.
caveats: Check the sqm per pack on your click vinyl. Pack sizes vary - adjust the pack coverage setting to match.
Most click vinyl does NOT need separate underlay. It has built-in backing. Check your product instructions before buying underlay.
Sheet vinyl roll widths matter. If your room is 3.2m wide and you buy from a 3m roll, you'll have a join. Buy from a 4m roll for seamless.
Subfloor prep can be a significant extra cost. Plywood overlay or self-levelling compound adds materials and time.
L-shaped rooms need more waste (15% not 10%) for click vinyl.
Sheet vinyl adhesive varies. Some is loose-laid (no adhesive), some uses spray adhesive, some uses trowel-on. Check your product.
Heavy furniture dents vinyl permanently. Use furniture cups/pads under heavy items.
refittr_message: Coming in June - check Refittr for second-hand flooring guaranteed to fit your room.
